topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit me first, a sudden, sharp intrusion into the otherwise peaceful afternoon. I’d been walking along the edge of the woods, tracing the line where my childhood project used to stand. It was a young maple, barely a teenager when I’d painstakingly set its roots in that patch of soil. Now, there was only a raw, jagged stump where vibrant green should have been. My fingers traced the rough, splintered wood, a coldness creeping up my arms despite the mild temperature. A tightness settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I looked around, scanning the dense undergrowth, half-expecting to see someone who knew why this happened. Nothing. Just the silence, punctuated by the rustle of unseen things in the leaves above.

[story 2]

I stopped dead on the trail, my pace faltering. The clearing was wrong. Where the sturdy, familiar silhouette of the sapling had stood just last summer, there was only an exposed patch of disturbed soil and splintered wood. My heart began to thump a rapid, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I knelt down, brushing away the loose dirt, searching for any sign—a discarded branch, a fresh cut. My hands trembled slightly as I examined the base of the stump. It looked… abrupt. Too clean. A strange prickling sensation started at the base of my neck, urging me to move, to flee the spot, but my feet felt rooted to the ground. I stood up slowly, my gaze darting from the empty space to the surrounding shadows of the forest, a knot tightening in my stomach.

[story 3]

It was a Tuesday, and I was supposed to be pruning the perimeter fence, but I found myself drawn back to the forgotten corner of the yard. There, where I’d nurtured that little tree from a clumsy seedling, the earth was scraped bare. The trunk, which I remembered so vividly, was gone, leaving behind only a ragged scar on the ground. I stood there, hands shoved deep into my pockets, watching the afternoon light filter through the canopy, illuminating the devastation. A shallow gasp escaped my lips. I took a step back, my eyes wide, trying to process the sudden void where something familiar had been. The air felt thick, heavy, pressing down on my shoulders. I didn't move to call out; I just stood, absorbing the stark reality of the absence.
